results.possibleIdentity: Alan Campbell was a doctoral student in social psychology and a systems programmer at Columbia University during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is recognized for providing significant computer programming and experimental assistance for major social psychology research at Columbia, most notably contributing to the work of Richard Christie and Florence Geis in their 1970 publication, *Studies in Machiavellianism*. His technical expertise spanned several interdisciplinary fields, including systems programming, linguistics, phonology, and computer linguistics. Campbell was also associated with the Columbia School of Linguistics, a group founded by William Diver at Columbia University that emphasized a functionalist, sign-based approach to language analysis. He likely engaged with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which at the time facilitated collaborations between New York-based engineers and scientists and the artistic community.
